topics.name: AI-Powered Solopreneurship & Agentic Automation
details: **What is happening:** AI, particularly 'agentic AI,' is transforming how solopreneurs operate, allowing them to automate entire workflows, administrative tasks, content creation, and even customer service, effectively giving one person the output of a small team.

**Why it matters:** This trend directly addresses the core pain points of time scarcity and workload for solopreneurs, enabling them to work less while earning more by leveraging technology to scale their impact and reclaim hours previously spent on repetitive tasks.

topics.name: Anti-Hustle & Lifestyle-First Business
details: **What is happening:** There's a significant cultural shift, particularly driven by Gen Z, rejecting traditional hustle culture in favor of sustainable work practices, prioritizing mental health, setting boundaries, and designing businesses around personal well-being rather than relentless output.

**Why it matters:** FieldWork's 'Live Offline' ethos directly aligns with this desire for a business that supports a fulfilling life, offering strategies to achieve financial success without sacrificing personal time, health, or relationships.

topics.name: Solopreneur Economy Boom & Escaping 9-to-5
details: **What is happening:** A record number of individuals are choosing solopreneurship, driven by a desire to escape the traditional 9-to-5, gain control over their work and income, and build businesses that offer greater flexibility and autonomy.

**Why it matters:** This trend signifies a large and growing audience actively seeking alternative paths to work, making FieldWork's promise of 'escaping the 9-to-5 grind' highly relevant and timely for aspiring and current solopreneurs.

topics.name: Reclaiming Time & Sustainable Work-Life Integration
details: **What is happening:** Solopreneurs are actively seeking strategies and tools to reclaim their time, establish clear boundaries, and build businesses that support a sustainable work-life integration rather than leading to burnout.

**Why it matters:** FieldWork directly addresses the goal of 'reclaiming their time' and 'working less while earning more' by providing frameworks and automation strategies that enable founders to achieve greater efficiency and create a business that serves their life.

topics.name: Digital Detox & Intentional Offline Living
details: **What is happening:** Consumers and solopreneurs are increasingly seeking to reduce screen time and engage in intentional digital detox to combat digital fatigue, improve mental well-being, and foster deeper real-world connections.

**Why it matters:** This trend is a direct call to action for FieldWork's 'Live Offline' component, providing a strong hook for content that helps solopreneurs create boundaries with technology and prioritize real-world experiences and relationships.

topics.name: Building Lean, Automated One-Person Companies
details: **What is happening:** Solopreneurs are leveraging modular tech stacks, AI, and automation to build lean, resilient businesses with minimal overhead, allowing them to scale revenue and impact without scaling complexity or needing to hire employees.

**Why it matters:** FieldWork can provide the blueprints and strategies for building these efficient, automated systems, demonstrating how solopreneurs can achieve significant business growth and financial freedom as a 'one-person company'.

topics.name: Micro-Offers & Productized Knowledge
details: **What is happening:** Solopreneurs are finding success by creating and selling 'micro-offers' or productized services—small, highly specific digital products, templates, or short sprints—that deliver clear outcomes quickly, allowing them to diversify income streams without increasing their workload.

**Why it matters:** FieldWork can teach solopreneurs how to identify their expertise, package it into scalable digital products, and leverage automation to sell these micro-offers, directly contributing to the goal of 'earning more while working less'.

topics.name: Time & Location Freedom
details: **What is happening:** Solopreneurs are increasingly prioritizing the ability to work from anywhere, seeking geographical and time freedom to travel, live abroad, or simply have more flexibility in their daily lives, often enabled by remote-first business models.

**Why it matters:** This aligns perfectly with FieldWork's 'Build Online. Live Offline.' mantra, offering solutions for solopreneurs to design businesses that support a nomadic or location-independent lifestyle, allowing them to spend more time outside and offline.

posts.hook: Nothing better than catching giant tuna with the boys on some deserted islands…
posts.creator: wadepapenfus
posts.headline: Systems For The Offline Life
posts.angle: Showcase the ultimate 'why' behind building systems. Frame a moment of being fully offline and present as the direct, intentional result of building an automated online business, not as a vacation or lucky break.
hooks: Turns out the best part of building an online business is being offline.
My business ran itself today. I went for a hike.
This is what my AI employees are working for.
I didn't check my email once today. This is why.
Built a business that doesn't need me. So I can be here.
talking_points: Turns out the best part of building an online business is being completely offline.
For years, I thought scaling a business meant more screen time. More meetings, more notifications, more time chained to a desk.
I was wrong. The real goal isn't just to build something that makes money.
The goal is to build systems that run without you. Engines that work for you, not because of you.
An AI employee that nurtures leads at 2 a.m. An automated system that answers 90% of customer questions in your voice, while you're nowhere near a signal.
This isn't about getting lucky or taking a vacation. It's about intentional design.
You build the engines once, so they can run forever. That's how you buy back your time.
This is the entire idea behind FieldWork. We build the online infrastructure so you can get back to your life offline.
If you want to build a business that funds your life instead of consuming it, comment 'FieldWork' below. I'll send you the details.
posts.body_copy: Turns out the best part of building an online business is being completely offline. For years, I thought scaling a business meant more screen time. More meetings, more notifications, more time chained to a desk. I was wrong. The real goal isn't just to build something that makes money. The goal is to build systems that run without you. Engines that work for you, not because of you. An AI employee that nurtures leads at 2 a.m. An automated system that answers customer questions in your voice, while you're nowhere near a signal. This isn't about getting lucky or taking a vacation. It's about intentional design. You build the engines once, so they can run forever. That's how you buy back your time. This is the entire idea behind FieldWork. We build the online infrastructure so you can get back to your life offline. If you want to build a business that funds your life instead of consuming it, comment 'FieldWork' below. I'll send you the details.
script: Hook: The best part of online business is being offline.
Problem: The false belief that scaling means more screen time.
Solution: Build systems and engines that run without you.
Proof: Give concrete examples of automated tasks.
Philosophy: This is intentional design, not a vacation.
Offer: This is the core principle of the FieldWork program.
CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' to learn how.
guidance: Film this entirely outdoors. Hiking, by a campfire, in the back of your truck. No office shots.
Start with epic, quiet B-roll of the scenery before you start talking to establish the 'offline' vibe.
Use clean, bold text overlays for key phrases like 'Build Online. Live Offline.' and 'Systems buy back your time.'
Keep the tone calm and grounded, like you're sharing a realization, not pitching a product.
End on a shot of the landscape with the call to action 'Comment "FieldWork"' clearly displayed on screen.

posts.creator: tomnoske
posts.headline: Systems Over Sweat
posts.angle: Directly counter the 'earned, not given' hustle mantra. Propose a better way: building a system once that buys back your time, instead of earning every single dollar with your sweat.
hooks: Hustle is rented. Systems are owned.
They say 'earned, not given'. I think that's a trap.
My business runs without me. Not because I earn it every day.
Sweat equity is the worst kind of equity.
Stop earning it. Start building it.
talking_points: Everyone loves the phrase 'earned, not given'. And it feels right.
But it's a trap. It's a trap that chains your time directly to your income.
If you stop working, you stop earning. That's not freedom. That's a high paying job.
I don't want a business I have to earn from every single day. I want a business I built, once.
A business that runs on systems, not my sweat. A system that works while I'm on a hike with my family.
It sends the emails. It qualifies the leads. It nurtures the customers. 24/7.
That's the whole point of FieldWork. We build online so we can live offline.
You build the engines that run without you, so you can get back out in the field.
That isn't earned fresh every morning. It was built the right way.
If you're done earning it and ready to build it, comment 'FieldWork'. I'll send you the details.
posts.body_copy: Everyone loves the phrase 'earned, not given'. And it feels true. But it's a trap. It's a trap that chains your income directly to your time. If you stop working, you stop earning. That isn't freedom. That's just a high paying job you created for yourself. I don't want a business I have to earn from every single day. I want a business I built. Once. A business that runs on systems, not my sweat. One that sends emails, qualifies leads, and nurtures customers while I'm on a hike with my family. That is the whole point. To build something online so you can get back to living offline. The goal isn't to hustle forever. The goal is to build the engines that run without you, so you can get back out in the field. That freedom isn't earned fresh every morning. It was built, the right way. If you're done earning it and you're ready to build it, comment 'FieldWork' below. I'll send you the details.
script: Challenge the 'earned, not given' hustle mantra.
Expose the trap of trading time for money.
Introduce systems as the alternative to daily hustle.
Define what a system does: works while you live.
Connect this to the FieldWork philosophy: Build Online, Live Offline.
End with a clear call to action.
guidance: Film this as a direct, talking-head shot. Warm, natural lighting. Make it feel grounded, not slick.
Use B-roll that contrasts the concept. Show quick cuts of you hiking, skiing, or with family, juxtaposed with a brief shot of an automation workflow on a laptop.
Open with a bold text hook on screen: 'Hustle is rented. Systems are owned.'
Keep on-screen text minimal but impactful. Use it to emphasize key phrases like 'Build Online. Live Offline.'
End with a simple, clean text card: 'Comment "FieldWork"'

posts.hook: Where could you be six months from today?
posts.creator: tomnoske
posts.headline: Your Next Six Months
posts.angle: Frame the 'where could you be' question not as a vague dream, but as the direct result of a specific choice. Contrast the path of continued hustle with the path of building a system that buys back your time.
hooks: Where will you be in six months? More importantly, where will your business be?
Six months from now, you can be in the exact same place. Or you can be free.
Let's talk about the next six months. Path one is hustle. Path two is a system. What's it going to be?
Most people overestimate what they can do in a day. And underestimate what a real system can do in six months.
Your business in six months. Still run by you. Or run by a system you built once.
talking_points: Everyone is asking where you could be in six months. But they miss the real question.
The real question is: will your business still need you for every single thing?
Because if it does, it doesn't matter if you're in Bali or your basement. You're still trapped.
The next six months can be more of the same. More DMs, more emails, more late nights. Chained to the desk.
Or, you can spend a fraction of that time building systems. AI employees that handle your marketing, sales, and operations while you sleep.
You build the engines once. Then you get to go back to the field. Back to your life.
That's the whole idea. Build online, so you can live offline.
If you want a blueprint for a business that runs itself, comment the word 'FieldWork' below. I'll send it over.
posts.body_copy: Where could you be six months from today? It's the wrong question. The real question is, will your business still need you to answer every email and post every story? Because if the answer is yes, you're not free. You're just a highly paid employee at a company you own. The next six months can look exactly like the last six. More hustle. More screen time. Or you can make a different choice. You can spend a fraction of that time installing systems. Building AI employees that run your marketing and sales in your voice, 24/7. You build the engine once. Then you get to return to the field. That's the promise. Build online so you can live offline. If you want the roadmap for the next six months to look fundamentally different from the last, comment the word 'FieldWork' below. I'll send you the details.
script: Hook: Reframe the 'six months' question around business dependency.
Problem: If the business needs you for everything, you're trapped.
Contrast: The hustle path vs. the systems path.
Solution: Install AI and automation to run the business for you.
Vision: Build the engine once, then 'live offline'.
CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' for the roadmap.
guidance: Film as a direct-to-camera talking head, preferably in a natural or workshop setting, not a sterile office.
Use B-roll that contrasts the two paths. Quick cuts of looking stressed at a laptop vs. shots of hiking, fishing, or being present with family.
On-screen text should be minimal and bold. Use it to emphasize key concepts like 'HUSTLE vs. SYSTEM' and the final CTA.
Pacing should be calm and confident. This isn't a hype video. It's a clear, authoritative choice you're offering.
End on a static shot of the call to action on screen: Comment 'FIELDWORK'.

posts.creator: tomnoske
posts.headline: Systems Beat Skill. Every Time.
posts.angle: Directly mirror the competitor's structure. Argue that a founder's raw skill is the 'content,' but without the right systems (the 'packaging'), it just leads to burnout. The system is the leverage point that buys back their freedom.
hooks: Systems > Skill.
An incredible founder with no systems is just an employee.
Your expertise doesn't matter if you're the bottleneck.
Stop perfecting your craft. Start building your engine.
You can be the best in the world at what you do and still be trapped.
talking_points: Systems are more important than skill.
An incredible founder with average systems will burn out. They are trapped by their own business.
An average founder with incredible systems will buy back their life. They are free.
Now, that's not to say that your skill doesn't matter. Of course it does.
Great skill, combined with a great system, is how you build a business that serves your life. Not the other way around.
But if you've got limited time, and you're going to place your focus anywhere, it has to be on the system.
The system is the engine that works when you're not.
It's the only way to build online so you can live offline.
That's what we install inside my program, FieldWork.
If you want to build the engine that buys back your time, comment FIELDWORK and I'll send you the details.

But if you have limited time, you have to focus on the highest leverage point first. That's the system. It's the engine that runs while you're in the field, doing what you love. Present the problem: great skill with no systems leads to burnout.
Present the alternative: average skill with great systems leads to freedom.
Add the nuance: The goal is great skill AND great systems.
State the priority: If time is limited, build the system first.
CTA for FieldWork to learn how to build the system.
guidance: Use on-screen text for the entire script, timed to your speech. The first three seconds should just be the text 'Systems > Skill.' on a clean background.
Shoot as a simple, direct-to-camera talking head. Chest-up, warm lighting. Make it feel personal and grounded.
At the line '...while you're in the field,' quickly cut to 1-2 seconds of B-roll showing you outdoors, hiking, or with family. Then cut back.
Keep the pace steady and confident. The power is in the clarity of the argument, not high energy.
For the call to action, point down towards the comments section as the text 'Comment: FIELDWORK' appears on screen.

posts.hook: I locked my 9-person sales team in a cupboard for 90 days.⁠
posts.creator: takimoore
posts.headline: I Fired Myself For 30 Days
posts.angle: Frame this as the ultimate test of the 'Build Online, Live Offline' philosophy. The angle is proving that a business built on systems, not hustle, can thrive even when the founder completely disconnects.
hooks: I fired myself for 30 days.
I took a 30-day vacation to see if my business would die.
I deleted email and Slack from my phone for an entire month.
For 30 days, I did zero work. No calls. No emails. No content.
I went completely offline for 30 days to prove a point.
talking_points: I fired myself for 30 days.
Full pay. But zero work. No emails. No DMs. No checking in on leads. I went completely offline.
It felt totally wrong. Like I was abandoning the business.
But it proved the most important lesson I know: your business doesn't need more of your time.
It needs better systems. It needs engines that run without you.
That's the whole point. To build it so you can get back out in the field.
That month, the systems I built booked 17 calls on their own.
They nurtured over 6,000 people on my email list, in my voice.
And they closed over $30,000 in new business while I was nowhere near a laptop.
No hustle. No burnout. No being chained to a desk wondering if things will break.
If you want to see how to install the engines that buy back your time, comment FIELDWORK below.
posts.body_copy: I fired myself for 30 days. Full pay. But I went completely offline. No emails. No DMs. No checking in on leads. It felt totally wrong. Like I was abandoning the whole thing. But it proved the most important point about building a business today. It doesn't need more of your time. It needs better systems. It needs engines that run without you, so you can get back to your life. That month, the AI systems I installed booked 17 calls on their own. They nurtured 6,000 people on my email list. They closed over thirty thousand dollars in new business while I was out in the field. No hustling. No being chained to my laptop. No wondering if it would all fall apart. Just systems. If you want to see how to build the engines that buy back your time, comment FIELDWORK below and I'll send you the details.
script: Hook: The 30-day 'fire myself' experiment.
Admission: It felt wrong but proved a vital point.
Thesis: Your business needs systems, not more of your time.
Proof: Specific metrics achieved while offline ($30k, 17 calls).
Benefit: Freedom from the laptop and hustle culture.
CTA: Comment FIELDWORK for the system details.
guidance: Shoot this as a direct-to-camera, talking head video. Medium shot, in a natural setting like your home office or an outdoor space to match the 'offline' vibe.
Splice in 2-3 short b-roll clips of you actually 'offline': hiking, with family, working on a vehicle, or just enjoying nature.
Use bold, simple on-screen text to emphasize key phrases and numbers as you say them: 'FIRED MYSELF FOR 30 DAYS', 'NO EMAILS', '$30,000'.
End with a clear, full-screen text card for the call to action: 'Comment: FIELDWORK'.

posts.hook: Part 3 of 5 — You do not have to force pay-in-full to build a great coaching bus
posts.creator: takimoore
posts.headline: Your Business Is Not The Finish Line
posts.angle: Contrast the hustle culture belief that the business is the end goal with the FieldWork philosophy that the business is a tool to build a better life. The reel should feel like a permission slip to stop building a cage and start building an engine for freedom.
hooks: You do not have to work 80 hours a week to build a great online business.
Most founders treat their business like the finish line.
Your business is not the prize. Your life is.
A lot of people think the goal is just to build a bigger business.
Stop building a business that needs you 24/7.
talking_points: A lot of people treat building the business like the finish line.
It's not.
The business is the start.
If your goal is to build online so you can live offline... then the point isn't just to make more revenue.
It's to build systems that run without you. Engines that create value while you're on a hike, or with your family.
People who get results from your systems, not just from your time.
That changes how you design your products.
That changes the marketing you do.
That changes how you think about your entire day.
There isn't one right way to be a founder. There's only the way that builds the life you actually want to live.
If you want to install the systems that buy your time back, comment 'FieldWork' below. I'll send you the details.
posts.body_copy: A lot of people treat building the business like the finish line. It's not. The business is the start. If your whole reason for being online is so you can live offline, then the goal isn't just to collect revenue. It's to build systems that run without you. Engines that deliver value while you're on a hike. Automation that works while you're with your family. This creates a business built on real results, not just your personal time. That changes how you design your products. That changes how you do your marketing. That changes how you think about your entire business model. There isn't one right way to be a founder. There's only the way that builds the life you actually want to live. If you want help installing the systems that buy your time back, comment 'FieldWork' below. I'll send you the details.
script: Hook: The business isn't the finish line.
Reframe: It's the starting line for your life.
The Real Goal: Build systems that run without you.
The Consequence: This changes how you build, market, and think.
The Punchline: Build the life you want, not just the business.
The CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' for the system.
guidance: Film as a simple, direct-to-camera talking head. Keep the background clean, maybe with an outdoor element visible.
Use bold, minimalist on-screen text to punch up the key lines: 'The business is the START.', 'That changes how you...', and 'Build Online. Live Offline.'
Intersperse 2-3 quick cuts of b-roll showing the 'offline' life: hiking, by a fire, with family. This visually proves the point.
For the final shot, hold on the CTA text 'Comment "FieldWork"' on screen for at least 3 seconds as you finish speaking.

ideas.title: Turns out the best part of building an online business is being offline.

hooks: Turns out the best part of building an online business is being offline.
My business ran itself today. I went for a hike.
This is what my AI employees are working for.
I didn't check my email once today. This is why.
Built a business that doesn't need me. So I can be here.
talking_points: Turns out the best part of building an online business is being completely offline.
For years, I thought scaling a business meant more screen time. More meetings, more notifications, more time chained to a desk.
I was wrong. The real goal isn't just to build something that makes money.
The goal is to build systems that run without you. Engines that work for you, not because of you.
An AI employee that nurtures leads at 2 a.m. An automated system that answers 90% of customer questions in your voice, while you're nowhere near a signal.
This isn't about getting lucky or taking a vacation. It's about intentional design.
You build the engines once, so they can run forever. That's how you buy back your time.
This is the entire idea behind FieldWork. We build the online infrastructure so you can get back to your life offline.
If you want to build a business that funds your life instead of consuming it, comment 'FieldWork' below. I'll send you the details.

ideas.title: My business, after I ghosted it for 14 days straight.

ideas.angle: Mirror the structure by juxtaposing a major personal 'disruption' (going completely offline) with a calm confidence in your niche 'obsession' (the automated business). The punchline isn't a joke, but the core promise of FieldWork: a business built so well it runs without you.
hooks: My business, after I ghosted it for 14 days straight.
Me, a week into a trip with zero cell service.
My reaction when someone asks if I'm 'putting out fires' while I'm on vacation.
POV: You built a business you don't have to babysit.
The moment you realize your vacation isn't costing you money. It's making it.
talking_points: This is me a week into a trip with zero cell service.
Most founders would be panicking right now.
They'd be hunting for a single bar of wifi just to check emails and stomp out the latest fire.
My business is fine. In fact, it closed two new deals this morning.
That's because I don't have a collection of tools. I have a system.
While I'm out here, an AI employee is handling support tickets in my voice.
An automated sequence is nurturing new leads that come in from my ads.
My content is still posting on schedule. My calendar is booking calls for when I get back.
This isn't an accident. It's a design. You build an online business so you can live your life offline.
If you want to learn how to install these systems that run without you, I show you everything inside FieldWork.
Just comment 'FieldWork' below and I'll send you the details.

ideas.title: Hustle is rented. Systems are owned.
ideas.headline: Systems Over Sweat
ideas.angle: Directly counter the 'earned, not given' hustle mantra. Propose a better way: building a system once that buys back your time, instead of earning every single dollar with your sweat.
hooks: Hustle is rented. Systems are owned.
They say 'earned, not given'. I think that's a trap.
My business runs without me. Not because I earn it every day.
Sweat equity is the worst kind of equity.
Stop earning it. Start building it.
talking_points: Everyone loves the phrase 'earned, not given'. And it feels right.
But it's a trap. It's a trap that chains your time directly to your income.
If you stop working, you stop earning. That's not freedom. That's a high paying job.
I don't want a business I have to earn from every single day. I want a business I built, once.
A business that runs on systems, not my sweat. A system that works while I'm on a hike with my family.
It sends the emails. It qualifies the leads. It nurtures the customers. 24/7.
That's the whole point of FieldWork. We build online so we can live offline.
You build the engines that run without you, so you can get back out in the field.
That isn't earned fresh every morning. It was built the right way.
If you're done earning it and ready to build it, comment 'FieldWork'. I'll send you the details.

A business that runs on systems, not my sweat. One that sends emails, qualifies leads, and nurtures customers while I'm on a hike with my family. That is the whole point. ideas.title: Where will you be in six months? More importantly, where will your business be?
ideas.headline: Your Next Six Months
ideas.angle: Frame the 'where could you be' question not as a vague dream, but as the direct result of a specific choice. Contrast the path of continued hustle with the path of building a system that buys back your time.
hooks: Where will you be in six months? More importantly, where will your business be?
Six months from now, you can be in the exact same place. Or you can be free.
Let's talk about the next six months. Path one is hustle. Path two is a system. What's it going to be?
Most people overestimate what they can do in a day. And underestimate what a real system can do in six months.
Your business in six months. Still run by you. Or run by a system you built once.
talking_points: Everyone is asking where you could be in six months. But they miss the real question.
The real question is: will your business still need you for every single thing?
Because if it does, it doesn't matter if you're in Bali or your basement. You're still trapped.
The next six months can be more of the same. More DMs, more emails, more late nights. Chained to the desk.
Or, you can spend a fraction of that time building systems. AI employees that handle your marketing, sales, and operations while you sleep.
You build the engines once. Then you get to go back to the field. Back to your life.
That's the whole idea. Build online, so you can live offline.
If you want a blueprint for a business that runs itself, comment the word 'FieldWork' below. I'll send it over.

Or you can make a different choice. You can spend a fraction of that time installing systems. Building AI employees that run your marketing and sales in your voice, 24/7. You build the engine once. Then you get to return to the field. That's the promise. Build online so you can live offline. Solution: Install AI and automation to run the business for you.
Vision: Build the engine once, then 'live offline'.
CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' for the roadmap.

Use B-roll that contrasts the two paths. Quick cuts of looking stressed at a laptop vs. shots of hiking, fishing, or being present with family.
On-screen text should be minimal and bold. Use it to emphasize key concepts like 'HUSTLE vs. SYSTEM' and the final CTA.
Pacing should be calm and confident. This isn't a hype video. It's a clear, authoritative choice you're offering.

ideas.title: Systems > Skill.
ideas.headline: Systems Beat Skill. Every Time.
ideas.angle: Directly mirror the competitor's structure. Argue that a founder's raw skill is the 'content,' but without the right systems (the 'packaging'), it just leads to burnout. The system is the leverage point that buys back their freedom.
hooks: Systems > Skill.
An incredible founder with no systems is just an employee.
Your expertise doesn't matter if you're the bottleneck.
Stop perfecting your craft. Start building your engine.
You can be the best in the world at what you do and still be trapped.
talking_points: Systems are more important than skill.
An incredible founder with average systems will burn out. They are trapped by their own business.
An average founder with incredible systems will buy back their life. They are free.
Now, that's not to say that your skill doesn't matter. Of course it does.
Great skill, combined with a great system, is how you build a business that serves your life. Not the other way around.
But if you've got limited time, and you're going to place your focus anywhere, it has to be on the system.
The system is the engine that works when you're not.
It's the only way to build online so you can live offline.
That's what we install inside my program, FieldWork.
If you want to build the engine that buys back your time, comment FIELDWORK and I'll send you the details.

But if you have limited time, you have to focus on the highest leverage point first. That's the system. It's the engine that runs while you're in the field, doing what you love. ideas.title: If your business can't run without you for two weeks, you don't own a business. You own a job.
ideas.headline: Your Business Is Not Your Life
ideas.angle: Position the business as a tool, not the destination. The goal isn't just to build a successful company, but to build an engine that buys back your time, allowing you to live a full life offline.
hooks: If your business can't run without you for two weeks, you don't own a business. You own a job.
If you're working more now than you did at your 9-to-5, you didn't build a business. You built a prison.
Most founders build a business that chains them to a desk. The smart ones build one that sends them into the field.
The goal isn't to make more money to buy more stuff. The goal is to build an engine that buys back your time.
If you're the bottleneck in your business, you don't need a bigger team. You need a smaller, smarter engine.
talking_points: If your business can't run for two weeks without you, you don't own a business. You own a high stress job with unlimited vacation days you can never take.
We all get into this for freedom. Freedom of time, freedom of location. But most of us just end up building a prison with better branding.
The problem is we build businesses that depend on our time. Every new client, every new sale, every support ticket demands a piece of you.
That model doesn't scale. It just burns you out.
The reframe is this: Your business is not your life. It's the engine that should fund your life.
You do that by building systems. AI employees. Automated workflows. Things that run without you.
Imagine an AI that handles your sales DMs at 2am. Or an automated onboarding that welcomes new clients while you're on a hike with your family.
That's the entire philosophy behind FieldWork. We build online so we can live offline.
It's not about hustle. It's about building the engine once, so you can return to the field.
If you're an expert, a coach, or a founder who wants to get their time back, I'm building a small group for this. DM me the word 'FIELD' and I'll send you the details.
ideas.body_copy: If your business can't run without you for two weeks, you don't own a business. You own a high stress job with unlimited vacation days you can never take. We all get into this for freedom. But most of us end up building a prison with better branding, chained to a desk. The problem is we build businesses that depend on our constant time and energy. That model doesn't scale. It just burns you out. The reframe is this: Your business is not your life. It's the engine that should fund your life. You do this by building systems. AI employees and automated workflows that run without you, while you sleep, while you're on a hike. This is the entire philosophy behind my program, FieldWork. We build online so we can live offline. It's not about hustle. It's about building the right engine once, so you can return to the field for good. If you're a founder or an expert who wants to get their time back, DM me the word 'FIELD' and I'll send you the details.
script: Hook: Your business is a high stress job, not a freedom machine.
The Problem: Most businesses are built to consume your time, not free it.
The Reframe: A business should be an engine that funds your life.
The Mechanism: Use AI and automation to build systems that run without you.
The Philosophy: Build Online. Live Offline.
The Offer: The FieldWork program teaches you how.
CTA: DM 'FIELD' for info.
guidance: Film as a direct-to-camera piece. Medium shot, clear audio. Consider filming outdoors or in a space with a window view to reinforce the 'offline' theme.
Use B-roll of you hiking, traveling, or with family. Juxtapose these 'offline' moments with screen recordings of automated systems running.
On-screen text should emphasize key phrases: 'You own a job.', 'Build an Engine.', and the final call to action.
The pacing should be steady and confident. No hype. Just a clear, grounded explanation of a better way to build.
End with a clean, full-screen text card: DM 'FIELD'
ideas.framework: Problem Reframing (If You X, You Need Y)

details.Angle: Position the business as a tool, not the destination. The goal isn't just to build a successful company, but to build an engine that buys back your time, allowing you to live a full life offline.
details.Hooks: If your business can't run without you for two weeks, you don't own a business. You own a job. | If you're working more now than you did at your 9-to-5, you didn't build a business. You built a prison. | Most founders build a business that chains them to a desk. The smart ones build one that sends them into the field.
details.Framework: Problem Reframing (If You X, You Need Y)

ideas.title: $220M for 117 brands later, and the goal was never the money.
ideas.headline: The Goal Was Never The Money
ideas.angle: Connect the '$220M across 117 brands' figure to the core FieldWork message. Frame the money not as the goal, but as the proof that building systems is the key to buying back your time and living offline.
hooks: $220M for 117 brands later, and the goal was never the money.
I helped generate $220M. But what I really bought was my time.
Everyone sees the $220M figure. They miss the real point entirely.
The goal was never to hit nine figures. It was to build a business I could walk away from.
I didn't build a business to get rich. I built it to get free.
talking_points: I've helped generate over $220M in revenue across 117 different brands. But it was never, ever about the money.
It was always about time. It was always about freedom.
In 2018, I went for a hike in Alaska. I came back to my van and everything I owned was gone. My window was smashed, and so was my business model.
In that moment, I didn't want to be a millionaire. I just wanted to build something that couldn't be stolen.
Something that a smashed window couldn't break. A system.
So I stopped chasing clients and started building engines. Automated systems that run without me.
The crazy growth that followed wasn't the goal. It was the byproduct.
The real win isn't the revenue. It's being able to spend weeks offline, knowing the work is still getting done.
If you feel chained to your business right now, this is your sign.
It's time to build online, so you can finally live offline. If you want the roadmap, just comment 'FieldWork' below.
ideas.body_copy:
I've helped generate over $220M in revenue across 117 brands. But it was never about the money. It was always about time.

In 2018, I went for a hike in Alaska. When I came back, my van window was smashed. Everything I owned was gone. My business was gone.

I didn't want to be a millionaire then. I just wanted to build something that couldn't be stolen. Something resilient. A system.

So I stopped chasing projects. I started building automated engines that run without me, 24/7.

The revenue that came after was just a byproduct. The real win is the freedom. The ability to disappear into the mountains for a week, knowing the work is still getting done.

This is your sign. If you feel chained to your desk, there is another way.

You build your business to serve your life, not the other way around. That's the whole point. Build online, so you can live offline.

If you want the roadmap we use, just comment 'FieldWork' below.
script: Hook with the $220M figure, then pivot to time.
Tell the Alaska story of losing everything.
Reveal the real goal: building resilient systems.
Frame financial success as a byproduct of freedom.
State the 'Build Online, Live Offline' philosophy.
Position this video as a 'sign' for burnt-out founders.
CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' for the roadmap.
guidance: Film as a simple, direct-to-camera talking head. Keep the background clean, maybe outdoors or in a workshop to feel grounded.
B-roll: Use quick cuts of your travel footage. The van, hiking, drone shots of mountains. Visually connect the 'offline' life to the 'online' work.
On-screen text: Use your standard font to overlay key phrases like '$220M Later...', '...it was about TIME.', and 'Build Online. Live Offline.'
Pacing should be calm and confident, not hyped. Let the story and the message carry the weight.
End with a clear, full-screen text card: 'Comment "FieldWork"'

details.Angle: Connect the '$220M across 117 brands' figure to the core FieldWork message. Frame the money not as the goal, but as the proof that building systems is the key to buying back your time and living offline.

creators.top_hook: Nothing better than catching giant tuna with the boys on some deserted islands…
Comment SHOWBIZ to get the deets for the workshop.
Life when you start monetizing your brain using ChatGPT. DM me the word “script”
